title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved minutes, acknowledged permits, discussed infrastructure updates including a $780,000+ grant for Birch Street reconstruction, and made several recommendations to the village board including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, contracts for asphalt crushing and road construction, and establishing $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharges.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road depression issues, and upcoming pavement/manhole repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street.

Staff reported road limits were lifted April 29th. Activities included mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributaries to prevent flooding at Crane Meadows, spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from an East Nick area storm, culvert repairs on Jones Street, and extensive equipment maintenance. Discussion addressed need for permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding issues near Zenzer Drive and Shorey Avenue.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, private well sampling, and lift station issues including pump maintenance and power failures. Significant clear water infiltration was identified - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water entering the sanitary system. Staff identified homeowners with sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers. The INI study with In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million, net operating income of $482,911 (consistent with 2024's $490,000), and 6% water loss. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure added. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions, noting grants help with one-time costs but recurring annual payments for maintenance and snow plowing are underfunded. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid of $4.23/ton for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site, with an estimated project total of $126,900 for 30,000 tons. Analysis showed this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 in savings for 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed establishing enforcement for illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Josh explained the significant costs from clear water infiltration. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100/day, distinguishing between unknowing violations (warning first) versus willful violations. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex builder needs temporary long lateral service to lot one on Radkey Avenue until the road is reconstructed. Committee added requirement that Radkey Avenue reconstruction occur no later than 2029, at which point the property owner will pay assessment for main extension. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Committee approved intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share for utility work (sanitary sewer and water main) is $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer intern. Motion to recommend both awards passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Authorize agreement for temporary water/sewer connection at 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project ($37,315)
Award construction services to Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project ($73,730)
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Consider moving Birch Street reconstruction from 2029 to 2028 due to grant funding
Investigate permanent pump solutions for flooding near Weston Avenue/Zenzer Drive area
Send water main flushing notices to residents for work beginning next week
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also received an update that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance for the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work Product Transmittals and Educational Presentations
New Business - Grant Request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from Staff and Commission Members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and unanimously elected as Chair. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by Jackson and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed without opposition.
The minutes from April 21st were approved. Motion was made and seconded by Renee. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the event, reporting 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year, and requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the Holiday Inn Express. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for detailed room data in the post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, noting the candidates were impressed with Marathon County. She mentioned meeting Anastasia, who won the competition, and described how candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical requirements and future expansion considerations.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to accommodate a gas pipeline easement running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity versus current staffing of 40 officers. An alternate storage shed is proposed but may be deferred based on bid costs. Commissioner Zagami suggested placing the shed further north to allow for future building expansion. Motion to approve with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the municipal well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and they will reach out to that property owner. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion of potential residential TIF districts and workforce housing was raised for future consideration, possibly to be incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, urging staff to waive excessive standards similar to accommodations made for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and compliance findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district implementation
Staff to report inspection findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts to commissioners
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on beyond approving April 1st meeting minutes. The meeting focused on familiarizing new supervisors with departmental missions, budgets, and organizational structures.

agenda.item: Call to order and notation of remote participants (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure for new members
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Health Department budget review and discussion
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli moved to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

Administrator Leonard explained the committee structure under section 2.04 sub 5, including jurisdiction over Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and affiliated entities like North Central Health Care and ADRC. He outlined reporting relationships with various statutory boards including Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, and Board of Health.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's 37.6 FTE staff serving four divisions: environmental health (1,200+ licenses for restaurants, pools, etc.), nursing/communicable disease, family health, and community health improvement. She highlighted the new homeless system response position, nurse family partnership program with first graduates, and public health vending machines offering free resources including tick kits. Administrator Leonard noted approximately half of the $5 million budget is tax levy with significant grant and fee revenue.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, describing the nearly 140 FTE department covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program in partnership with the state, noting significant outcomes addressing root causes of housing instability. The $22 million budget includes approximately $7 million in tax levy.

VSO Erin Galindo reported his 3.0 FTE office handled 8,149 interactions in 2025, up significantly from prior years. The office facilitated $44 million annually in VA benefits to local veterans plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. He discussed priorities including surviving spouse benefits, housing stability through Center for Veterans Issues partnership, and suicide prevention (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county have a VSO but offices cooperate across boundaries.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional organization serving Lincoln, Langlade, Marathon and Wood counties with about 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. Services include information/assistance, Meals on Wheels nutrition program, Medicare/Medicaid counseling, falls prevention, and entry point for Family Care/IRIS programs. He noted significant financial challenges with federal funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45% in four years, forcing service reductions. Marathon County contributes $413,135 in levy.

actionItems: North Central Healthcare will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
Next meeting confirmed for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Strategic plan review expected at upcoming meetings with goal of completion by December

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances regulating mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new Kennedy Park location. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals and committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment questioning the justification for the fees, stating the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb Clark supported the fees as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 both passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement where Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside fire departments will recognize each other's food truck inspections. A sticker system will indicate which department inspected a truck, eliminating redundant inspections across municipalities. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook. Several trustees wanted to remove specific language about health, safety, and welfare requirements for remote attendance, preferring broader discretion. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific justifications for remote attendance. The amendment passed 5-2 with Trustees Hermling and Lob Sorrell opposed. The original motion as amended also passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Both appointments were approved unanimously. The board discussed having committee applications shared in packets going forward. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings.

Board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new Kennedy Park location after losing their previous Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Trustee Lisa Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the history: the market grew from 25 stalls at the old municipal building to 42 at Camp Phillips (where vehicles couldn't accompany vendors) to 53 spots at Kennedy Park where vendors can keep vehicles. Beck questioned costs but staff confirmed the market operates without loss through vendor fees and grants. No action was taken; discussion only.

During public comment, Jim Pitel questioned why the engineering technician position remains unfilled while the village pays outside consultants. Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set is scarce in central Wisconsin, noting even consulting firms struggle to find local candidates and pull from southeastern Wisconsin offices.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained the business deals in precious metals and gold with large dollar transactions. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke twice. First, he criticized the village for spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants while the engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed, complained about lack of Kennedy Park information in meeting packets, and criticized the administrator's accountability. During the impact fee public hearing, he questioned the justification for fee increases, stating the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. No other public comments were offered.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units effective immediately
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Mobile food vendor regional inspection agreement ordinance adopted
E-bike/e-moto ordinance amendments adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Weston Avenue speed limit study to be completed and brought back to board
Committee and commission applications to be included in board packets going forward

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large executive committee members were elected (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and a resolution was passed to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution, which passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call and technical difficulties with meeting manager system
Committee appointments presentation and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Announcement of executive committee election results
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing perspective diversity, developing new supervisors as leaders, and accommodating supervisor preferences. He noted that over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large executive committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, noting she 'advocates for our county's smallest children and oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fees for Disaster Debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that caused flooding and tornado damage, particularly in Ringle. The state waived $13.10, Ringle waived $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to the commercial rate of approximately $36.94 per ton. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive all fees. Supervisor Rosenberg requested continued discussion at the Solid Waste Board. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak during the tipping fee discussion. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive all fees for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees and that private businesses have donated significant resources. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and more time for cleanup since many insurance adjusters have not yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved: out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved: social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved: reduced landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Supervisor Gao requested notification of future Solid Waste Board meetings

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and, after significant debate, approved a modified resolution to waive only reconnect fees for septic systems for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of modification to county zoning and pout septic fees for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Garrett Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, noting the governor declared a state of emergency. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future disasters. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to property owners affected by smaller disasters not receiving similar relief. Marilyn Bend asked about hookup costs for septic systems. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve the full resolution. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees for septic systems ($160 county fee), excluding other zoning and septic fees. The amendment passed, and the amended resolution was approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill facility, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges requiring on-site treatment by 2032 due to PFAS concerns, and significant capital expenses. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work helping Ringle tornado victims. Supervisor Drew thanked Hagen Busher for his passionate work and asked about grant applications; he explained they must reapply for 2027 funding after the 2026 application was deemed incomplete.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both county and city facilities. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generating $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement. Supervisor Drebeck confirmed horseback riding is still allowed at Big Eau Pleine Park.

actionItems: Rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential approved for James Offer and David Tract property in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in Town of McMillan - forwarded to County Board
Modified resolution to waive only septic system reconnect fees (not other zoning or septic fees) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 tornado forwarded to County Board for consideration
Towns recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect approved zoning changes

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and disc golf course tree removal. The committee also discussed ongoing graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the previous meeting were approved with a motion and second. The vote passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster presented a timeline document tracking the Kennedy Park master plan from approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. He clarified that the Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently at approximately $84-85,000 toward their $117,000 goal. Staff explained that village infrastructure improvements are on hold pending fundraising progress. The $8,700 referenced in financials was clarified as a Youth Baseball contribution for equipment. Friends of Kennedy Park plan a public check presentation to the Community Foundation at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photographs showing tree removal locations along the river and mat installation over gas pipelines. Dan explained that wood mats were used to protect two gas pipelines during equipment access, and the village retained some extra mats for potential future trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR pipeline) conducted site visits and walkthrough after the work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The presiding officer noted that resident Andy's objection to the program would be noted and that he was welcome to return for next month's discussion.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges in removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting that pressure washers cannot access the trail due to bridge limitations and soft ground. Discussion included potentially using anti-vandalism coatings or sealers, and organizing volunteer work days for maintenance. Committee member suggested community volunteers might help with seal coating, comparing to Friends of Kennedy Park model. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of proposed signage for Eau Claire River kayak launches. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Village of Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs include QR codes linking to river gauge data and Marathon County Parks Foundation donation URL. Funding comes from state grants through Marathon County Parks Foundation. The committee approved a motion to allow the village to install the signs, to be forwarded to the village board. Motion passed unanimously.

Director Oster reported aquatic center pool repairs were completed, filling began and should finish by end of next day. Dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed. Pool training scheduled for the weekend with rentals starting next Wednesday and opening day on June 6th. Staff also mentioned upcoming Mock Miller master plan process kickoff with Rettler and bike/pedestrian plan open house on June 11th.

publicComment: Two individuals offered public comment. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) opposed allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking remotely under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting as 'convoluted and misleading,' specifically challenging the $8,700 expenditure figure and asking about the $10,000 grant and Glow event proceeds, and suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Motion approved to allow installation of Eau Claire River kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations - forwarded to village board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning options for Eau Claire River Trail
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal to future meeting
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations, election administration, licensing, and tax collection. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April budget status report and TIF reports
discussions.body: Committee reviewed the April budget status report and TIF one and TIF two detail reports. Discussion focused on TIF closure timeline, with staff explaining bonds are due October 1st and they anticipate calling bonds in October/November. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, expected this summer. Staff confirmed final numbers look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Municipal Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office now has two full-time employees (Pam and Sarah) and one part-time employee (Leah), down from four full-time staff 12 years ago. Key topics covered included election administration (with Badger Books costing about $11,000 for four units), licensing (51 liquor licenses, bartender licenses), tax collection (84% of $22 million collected at the clerk's office counter), and pet licensing which was brought back in-house from Pet Data in 2025. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing) for 5,089 postal patrons. Discussion occurred about pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 for neutered animals due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at the new location has been going well. She has been present for the last three Saturdays from 6 a.m. for 6-7 hours helping vendors. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and some produce vendors with greenhouses. Committee members discussed parking concerns on Alderson (now allowed on west side only) and Newport streets, with some congestion observed during concurrent baseball and track events.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. This was noted as his last meeting.

actionItems: CDA resolution to call TIF bonds expected this summer, with bonds due October 1st
Staff to reach out to Ellers regarding bond closure process
Chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 p.m.

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, approving amendments to the nuisance ordinance for recommendation to the board, advancing an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, and authorizing a raze order for a dilapidated structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further staff work.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating 'my personal opinion is it's a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed. He raised concerns about ADA-compliant parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt, questioned the necessity of new fire inspections for food trucks, and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, noting some have been pending for years with no visible progress.

Roman/Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted that the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations across three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 made by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Committee reviewed final copy of nuisance ordinance amendments, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways). Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Committee extensively discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion covered whether electric scooters should be included in restrictions, with committee members noting middle schoolers commonly use them. After debate about over-regulation versus safety, motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Michael from BPW noted overnight restrictions don't help operations since plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am. Chief Hunt advocated keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. Committee discussed uniformity concerns and impacts on multifamily housing. Item deferred to July meeting for Michael to identify specific problem areas that need signage.

Committee discussed Mount Olive Church's request for a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Staff estimated cost at $2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs, passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a regional consortium (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer) has established mutual fire inspection recognition for food trucks. One inspection with a sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), properties on Ross Avenue requiring renumbering due to sequence issues, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing even number assignment. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having unusual addressing with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Staff requested authorization for raze order on structure abandoned since 2010-2014. The structure was previously considered for fire department training burn but was too deteriorated for safe exercise. Chickens were found being housed inside when a light was observed in the building. Current owners, who are up to date on taxes, prefer the village handle demolition with costs placed on tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park as 'terrible execution,' raising concerns about ADA parking compliance on grass, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, and expressing frustration with the age of some code enforcement cases that have shown no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended to Board of Trustees for approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition added recommended to Board of Trustees
Michael (BPW) to identify specific areas needing parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Michael (BPW) to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for three properties recommended to Board of Trustees
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
